body:
## UX goal
Provide a private, low-noise feedback loop where writers can collect precise chapter feedback, triage it quickly, and resolve it without breaking drafting flow. The experience should feel trustable (clear permissions), fast to scan, and revision-oriented rather than social.

## Information hierarchy
1. Share controls (comments on/off, link state, revoke/regenerate) at chapter level first so privacy state is always explicit.
2. Reader feedback context second: anchored comment thread grouped by scene/paragraph target with timestamp + reader alias.
3. Writer action layer third: resolve/reopen, mark planned follow-up, and filter by open/resolved/chapter.
4. General notes channel last for non-anchored feedback.

## Layout and components
- Chapter editor page: add a right-side `Feedback` panel in the existing sidebar slot used by `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` (`.sidebar`, `.sidebarSection`) so drafting canvas remains primary.
- In preview/share surface: keep read-only chapter body as main column; anchored comment markers appear inline in the margin with click-to-open thread drawer.
- Writer inbox view: toolbar + list pattern aligned with existing `Stories` toolbar/list structure (`src/app/stories/page.tsx`).
- Core UI elements:
  - `SharePreviewCard` (link status, expiry, comments toggle, regenerate/revoke)
  - `AnchorMarker` (inline pin with count badge)
  - `CommentThreadItem` (alias, timestamp, body, resolve state)
  - `FeedbackInboxFilters` (open/resolved/chapter)
  - `GeneralChapterNotes` section below anchored threads

## Interaction states
- Idle: show chapter text with subtle anchor markers and an always-visible feedback summary count.
- Loading: skeleton rows in inbox + placeholder markers in preview (reuse skeleton cadence from corkboard pulse style).
- Empty: two explicit variants:
  - No feedback yet (encourage sharing link)
  - No results for active filters (show `Clear filters` action)
- Error: inline, non-blocking error banners near affected surface (link action or comment action) with retry.
- Success: lightweight confirmation for comment submit/resolve/reopen/revoke (inline status text, not modal).

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: all markers, thread items, filter chips, resolve/reopen actions reachable via tab and operable with Enter/Space.
- Focus: visible 2px focus rings consistent with existing `--color-carbon-600` usage in inputs/buttons.
- Contrast: keep text/border pairings within approved grayscale tokens; avoid `--color-cement-400` for critical controls.
- Text clarity: action labels should be task language (`Resolve`, `Reopen`, `Add follow-up`) and avoid ambiguous icon-only controls.

## Visual style
- Stay within current grayscale design tokens from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- Recommended mapping:
  - Background surfaces: `--color-white`, `--color-polar-100`
  - Borders/dividers: `--color-fashion-300`
  - Primary text/actions: `--color-black-700`, `--color-carbon-600`
  - Secondary metadata (timestamps/aliases): `--color-elephant-500`
  - Muted helper text only: `--color-cement-400`
- Keep typography and spacing consistent with current shell/cards; no new accent colors in v1.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop (>=1024px): three-zone behavior when possible: chapter content, inline anchors, right feedback panel.
- Tablet (768-1023px): collapse to two zones; feedback panel toggles as slide-over to preserve reading width.
- Mobile (<768px): comments become stacked bottom sheet/panel; anchor selection scrolls to text block then opens thread below.
- Preserve one-handed quick actions on mobile: resolve/reopen and filter access within first viewport.

## Codebase alignment
- Reviewed current structure: `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/components/AppShell/*`, `src/components/ContentContainer/*`, `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`, `src/components/Corkboard/*`, `src/components/SceneCard/*`, `src/styles/tokens.css`, `docs/INSPIRATION.md`.
- Alignment call: use existing story page split layout (`editorMain` + `sidebar`) and tokenized CSS modules.
- Structural change needed: introduce explicit chapter preview/read-only route and feedback inbox route; current app has no share/comment routes or comment data primitives yet.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - App shell, header, content container, sidebar scaffolding
  - Toolbar/list visual pattern from `Stories` page
  - Focus styling conventions and skeleton animation approach
- New build:
  - Anchored comment marker + thread components
  - Share preview controls
  - Writer feedback inbox + filters
  - Comment state badges and resolve workflow affordances

## Feasibility notes
- Anchoring must survive text edits: design should support degraded fallback (`original anchor moved`) with jump-to-nearest block rather than silent loss.
- Reader identity should remain lightweight (alias + timestamp) and never imply account-level public profile in v1.
- Privacy constraint: preview must remain read-only and isolated from internal navigation/chrome.
- Filtering and resolve state should be deterministic and persistent across reload.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Anchor UI should plug into `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` editor surface without reducing core drafting readability.
- New feedback panel should follow existing sidebar dimensions from `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` and token spacing scale.
- Route-level additions should preserve `AppShell` structure (`src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`) and mobile navigation behavior.
- Any new controls should match existing button/input affordance style from `ViewModeSwitch`, `Stories` search/filter bar, and `SceneCard` select interactions.

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This is a strong differentiator if we keep it private-draft-first: anchored feedback on shared previews gives writers actionable revision input without turning the product into a public social platform. That positions us between Living Writer sharing and Inkitt community engagement, with a tighter workflow for serious draft iteration.

## Competitor evidence
- Living Writer supports sharing manuscripts/chapters via links and invitations with permission levels (view/comment/edit), but the docs emphasize sharing mechanics over an anchored feedback triage workflow: https://guides.livingwriter.com/share-manuscript-and-chapters
- Living Writerâ€™s own update notes indicate a feedback system is still being launched and collaboration comments are evolving, which leaves room for us to define a clearer writer-centric feedback inbox now: https://guides.livingwriter.com/whats-new/changelog/june-17-2025
- Inkitt strongly centers reader interaction through reviews and chapter comments/emojis in a community context; our opportunity is to deliver high-signal private beta feedback instead of engagement-first social loops: https://www.inkitt.com/writersblog/why-subscribe/
- Campfire highlights collaboration (invite collaborators to view/comment/edit), but broad co-writing and community positioning can be noisier than a focused beta-reader review lane for manuscript revision: https://www.campfirewriting.com/ and https://www.campfirewriting.com/pricing

## Risks / trade-offs
- Comment moderation/spam and abuse controls become necessary once external readers can comment.
- Anchoring comments to mutable text can create orphaned references after edits unless we define robust anchoring behavior.
- Scope can bloat into full collaboration if boundaries (no real-time co-authoring/public feed) are not enforced.

## Recommendation
Approve with a positioning constraint: optimize for private, high-signal revision loops (anchor + resolve + writer inbox) and explicitly avoid community/discovery mechanics in v1. Add acceptance language for anchor persistence behavior after manuscript edits to prevent UX regressions.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design

## Review Request

Requesting Market Research validation for differentiation and scope filter before engineering intake.

- Ticket: [BIN-71](/BIN/issues/BIN-71)
- Goal linkage: privacy-first trust layer for optional AI in drafting workflow
- Ask: approve/reframe with competitor-backed positioning

Assign to: Market Research
